coredata.dc:title: Metal–organic framework- and graphene quantum dot-incorporated nanofibers as dual stimuli-responsive platforms for day/night antibacterial bio-protection
coredata.dc:creator: Zou, Yucheng
Weng, Junjie
Qin, Zeyu
Zhang, Yipeng
Ji, Shengyang
Zhang, Hui
coredata.dc:description: Development of advanced antibacterial nanomaterials is important to prevent pathogenic microbial contamination and achieve bioprotection. Herein, we developed dual-stimuli-responsive nanoplatforms with efficient antibacterial activity based on electrospun polycaprolactone/hydroxypropyltrimethylammonium chloride chitosan (PQ) nanofiber membranes (NFMs) incorporated with naringenin-loaded zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (NAR@ZIF-8) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs). These NFM nanoplatforms could simultaneously be used as “smart” pH-responsive controlled-release and photocatalytic systems. NAR was encapsulated in the microporous structure of the ZIF-8 channel, with a loading capacity of 22.57%. NAR@ZIF-8- and GQD-incorporated PQ (PQZG) nanofibers exhibited enhanced thermal stability and mechanical properties. The release kinetics and microstructure of the nanocarriers demonstrated that the acid-sensitive cleavable crystal structure of ZIF-8 resulted in the pH-responsive release of NAR from PQZG NFMs. Moreover, the presence of GQDs significantly enhanced the photoinduced reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation by NFMs. pH-triggered NAR release and photoinduced ROS generation improved the antibacterial efficacy of PQZG NFMs, forming the synergetic chemical-photodynamic strategies of the dual stimuli-responsive nanoplatforms. Additionally, cytotoxicity experiments revealed that the PQZG NFMs were non-toxic and had good biocompatibility. Therefore, the designed composite nanomaterials combined with the two antibacterial strategies can aid in the development of biosafe nanofibrous membranes for antibacterial or multifunctional bio-protection.

head.ce:title: Metal–organic framework- and graphene quantum dot-incorporated nanofibers as dual stimuli-responsive platforms for day/night antibacterial bio-protection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of advanced antibacterial nanomaterials is important to prevent pathogenic microbial contamination and achieve bioprotection. Herein, we developed dual-stimuli-responsive nanoplatforms with efficient antibacterial activity based on electrospun polycaprolactone/hydroxypropyltrimethylammonium chloride chitosan (PQ) nanofiber membranes (NFMs) incorporated with naringenin-loaded zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (NAR@ZIF-8) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs). These NFM nanoplatforms could simultaneously be used as “smart” pH-responsive controlled-release and photocatalytic systems. NAR was encapsulated in the microporous structure of the ZIF-8 channel, with a loading capacity of 22.57%. NAR@ZIF-8- and GQD-incorporated PQ (PQZG) nanofibers exhibited enhanced thermal stability and mechanical properties. The release kinetics and microstructure of the nanocarriers demonstrated that the acid-sensitive cleavable crystal structure of ZIF-8 resulted in the pH-responsive release of NAR from PQZG NFMs. Moreover, the presence of GQDs significantly enhanced the photoinduced reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation by NFMs. pH-triggered NAR release and photoinduced ROS generation improved the antibacterial efficacy of PQZG NFMs, forming the synergetic chemical-photodynamic strategies of the dual stimuli-responsive nanoplatforms. Additionally, cytotoxicity experiments revealed that the PQZG NFMs were non-toxic and had good biocompatibility. Therefore, the designed composite nanomaterials combined with the two antibacterial strategies can aid in the development of biosafe nanofibrous membranes for antibacterial or multifunctional bio-protection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Emerging infectious diseases trigger public health emergencies worldwide [1,2]. Various emerging infectious disease outbreaks, such as the severe acute respiratory syndrome, coronavirus disease 2019, avian influenza, and Ebola virus outbreaks, have caused high mortality and severe economic disruption [3–5]. Pathogenic microbial contamination and biofilm infections are difficult to treat with common antibiotics. Multidrug-resistant bacteria are more harmful than drug-susceptible strains as they are resistant to most drugs [6]. The World Health Organization estimates that antibiotic-resistant bacteria will cause nearly 10 million annual deaths worldwide by 2050 [7]. Many advanced nanomaterials, including metallic nanoparticles, metal oxides, MXenes, graphene, quantum dots, and reticular porous materials, exhibit great potential to overcome antimicrobial resistance in the application of environments, biomedicine, and food safety [8–12].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are promising single-layer graphene materials that are a few nanometers in diameter [13]. GQDs have a large specific surface area, high solubility, and superior biocompatibility compared to traditional graphene and graphite oxide [14]. GQDs generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) under white light irradiation, leading to cell death in bacteria [15,16]. ROS, including singlet oxygen (1O2), superoxide anion (O2
−), and hydroxyl (OH) radicals, exhibit promising antimicrobial effects by causing oxidative damage to the cellular constituents of the pathogen [17,18]. Accordingly, GQDs have been used to develop advanced materials for wound dressing, fabrics, and antimicrobial photodynamic therapy (aPDT) [15,19,20]. GQD-driven aPDT does not cause pathogen resistance due to the non-specific apoptosis induced by ROS [21], providing a viable alternative to conventional antibiotic treatment.

Metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) are an emerging class of porous materials assembled from coordinated metal ions and organic ligands with ultrahigh porosity, large specific surface area, and tunable functionality [22]. Owing to their high biocompatibility, MOFs have attracted considerable attention for biomedical applications, particularly as antibacterial materials and drug delivery agents [23–25]. Generally, MOF-based materials mainly exhibit one or more of the following antimicrobial mechanisms: 1) MOFs perform antibacterial activity via the release of antibacterial metal ions (such as Ag, Cu, and Zn) during degradation [26], 2) MOFs with superior structures act as effective carriers for antimicrobial agents [27], 3) further functional modification of MOFs [28]. Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) is a subclass of MOFs that act as pH-sensitive drug carriers for stability under physiological conditions and degradation under acidic conditions [29]. Bim-Júnior et al. [30] reported that quercetin-loaded ZIF-8 exhibits acid-triggered drug release and antioxidant and antibacterial activities against Streptococcus mutans. Nabipour et al. [31] reported that ciprofloxacin-loaded ZIF-8 releases faster at pH 5.0 than at pH 7.4 and exhibits excellent antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli. Notably, the microenvironment is acidified (pH 5.5) after bacterial infection owing to the secretion of lactic and acetic acids during bacterial growth [32,33]. The pH-triggered ZIF-8 drug delivery system can be directly activated in response to the onset of a pathogenic infection. This pH-sensitive release behavior makes ZIF-8 suitable for the design of sophisticated drug delivery platforms for the on-demand release of drugs.

Naringenin (NAR) is a bioactive flavonoid found in grapes and citrus plants that exerts antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and anticancer effects [34]. However, NAR has low bioavailability and limited utilization owing to its low aqueous solubility and chemical instability [35]. ZIF-8 can be acted as a drug nanocarrier to encapsulate hydrophobic NAR molecules. In this study, we designed dual stimuli-responsive (antibacterial photoinactivation under light irradiation combined with pH-triggered on-demand drug release) nanofiber membranes (NFMs) incorporated with GQDs and NAR@ZIF-8 to achieve efficient antibacterial activity (Scheme 1 ). We further characterized GQDs, NAR@ZIF-8, and NFMs. Moreover, we investigated the ROS production, drug release behaviors, activities against S. aureus and E.coli bacteria, and in vitro cytotoxicities of the composite NFMs. Therefore, our on-demand antibacterial platform can be used to develop next-generation nanomaterials with uninterrupted day/night antibacterial activity.

Citric acid (99.5%) and Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (99%) were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NAR (97%), 2,2,2-trifluoroethanol (TFEA, 99.5%), 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF, 97%), hydroxypropyltrimethylammonium chloride chitosan (QCS, 98% deacetylated), and 2-methylimidazole (98%) were purchased from Macklin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Polycaprolactone (PCL; Mn = 80 kDa) was supplied by Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). E. coli (ATCC 25922) and S. aureus (CMCC 26003) were obtained from the China Center of Industrial Culture Collection (Beijing, China). All other chemicals and solvents were of analytical grade and purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

GQDs were synthesized using a bottom-up method via the direct pyrolysis of citric acid, as previously described [36]. In a typical procedure, 2 g citric acid monohydrate was put into a 20-mL beaker and heated to 200 °C using an oven. After heating for 30 min, orange liquids were obtained, indicating the formation of GQDs. Then, GQDs were dissolved in 100 mL of NaOH solution (10 mg mL−1) and filtered through cellulose filtration membranes (0.22 μm). The mixture was then dialyzed in a cellulose ester dialysis bag (100–500 Da; Spectrum Labs, USA) against ultrapure water for 72 h, and pure GQD powder was obtained via freeze-drying.

ZIF-8 and drug-loaded ZIF-8 were prepared as previously described [37], with slight modifications. Briefly, 0.2 g (0.67 mM) Zn(NO3)2·6H2O was dissolved in 0.8 mL of methanol. Then, 2 g (24.36 mM) of 2-methylimidazole was dissolved in 6 mL of methanol and added in Zn(NO3)2·6H2O dropwise with stirring. After stirring for 30 min, the product (ZIF-8) was collected via centrifugal separation (10 000 rpm, 10 min), washed thrice with methanol to remove the unreacted reagents, and freeze-dried for 12 h to obtain the ZIF-8 powder.

NAR@ZIF-8 was synthesized using a similar procedure. First, 4 mL of NAR methanol solution (2.5, 5, 7.5, and 10 mg mL−1) was added to the Zn(NO3)2·6H2O solution before mixing with the 2-methylimidazole solution with stirring for 5 min. All other steps were identical to those used for ZIF-8 synthesis.

As previously described [38], NAR@ZIF-8 (5 mg) was sufficiently decomposed by 250 μL of HCl (1 M) under vortex shaking and diluted by 2 mL ethanol. NAR concentration was determined via UV–vis absorbance measurements on a microplate reader (Infinite M200 Pro, TECAN, Switzerland) at 330 nm using the standard curve method (Fig. S1). NAR drug-loading capability (DLC) and drug-loading efficiency (DLE) were calculated using the following equations:
(1)

NAR @ ZIF‐ 8
× 100 %

PCL (85% w/w) and QCS (15% w/w) solutions were obtained by dissolving 0.8 g PCL and QCS (0.141 g) in a mixture of TFEA (10 mL) and an aqueous solution (3 mL) with stirring overnight. Then, NAR@ZIF-8 and GQDs were dispersed in the PCL/QCS solution at the same weight to obtain NAR@ZIF-8 and GQDs (5, 10, and 15 wt%) mixtures. Before electrospinning, the mixtures were ultrasonicated for 10 min, followed by vigorous stirring for 8 h to obtain a homogeneous distribution. Electrospinning was performed using an electrospinning equipment (YFSP-T; Yunfan Instrument, China) at a high voltage of 25 kV and a feed rate of 0.43 mL h−1. The distance between the needle tip and grounded rotating collector was 15 cm, and the rotational speed was 250 rpm. The electrospinning process was performed at 25 °C under 40–50% relative humidity. PCL/QCS nanofibers incorporated with 0, 5, 10, and 15 wt% NAR@ZIF-8 and GQDs were named PQ, PQZG5, PQZG10, and PQZG15, respectively.

A G300 field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM; Zeiss, Germany) equipped with an energy-dispersive spectrometer (EDS; BRUKER, Germany) and a JEM-1200EX transmission electron microscope (TEM; Jeol, Japan) were used to analyze the morphology and elemental distributions of the nanoparticles and nanofibers. Diameter distribution of nanofibers was calculated using Nano Measurer 1.2 by 100 random measurements. Functional groups were detected by the NICOLET iS50 Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA) at 4000 to 400 cm−1. Crystal structures of the synthesized materials were investigated using powder X-ray diffraction (XRD; D8 ADVANCE, BRUKER, Germany). Their elemental composition was determined using an Escalab 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS; Thermo Scientific, USA). Fluorescence spectra at different excitation wavelengths were recorded using an F-4500 fluorospectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). N2 adsorption–desorption analysis was conducted using an automated surface area and pore size analyzer (AUTOSORB-IQ2-MP; Quantachrome Instruments, USA). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed using a TGA Q500 instrument (Newcastle, USA). The water contact angle (WCA) was evaluated using an OCA20 contact angle goniometer (DataPhysics Instruments, Germany). Mechanical strengths of the nanofibrous mats were measured using a mechanical testing machine (Instron 5944, USA).

In a typical release system, 20 mg of NFMs (PQZG5, PQZG10, and PQZG15) was added into 10 mL phosphate-buffered saline (PBS; pH 5.0, 6.5, and 7.4) with constant stirring at 37 °C. The supernatant (0.2 mL) was collected at each scheduled time point and replaced with fresh PBS to detect the amount of NAR released using UV–vis absorbance measurements at 357 nm. The cumulative amount of NAR released was determined using the following equation:
(3)
NAR
release
( % )
=
M rel
M load
× 100 %
where Mrel is the amount of NAR released from PQZG NFMs and Mload is the total amount of NAR loaded in PQZG NFMs.

Photodynamic properties of the prepared electrospun NFMs were evaluated by detecting ROS generation upon irradiation, and DPBF was used as an ROS (1O2)-sensitive probe to determine ROS generation, as previously described [39]. A piece of 5 mg NFMs (PQ, PQZG5, PGZG 10, and PGZG15) was placed in a 10-mL vial containing DPBF acetonitrile solution (3 mL, 0.444 mmol/L). The vial was placed in the dark for 5 min to achieve the adsorption/desorption equilibrium of DPBF on PQZG NFMs. Subsequently, the mixture was irradiated with a xenon lamp (532 nm, 100 mW/cm2 fluence rate) for 10 min, and the absorbance of the DPBF solution at 360–500 nm was recorded every 2 min. Pure DPBF solution without NFMs was prepared by irradiation and used as the control.

Growth inhibition activities of PQZG NFMs against E. coli and S. aureus were determined using the turbidimetric method, as previously reported [40]. Different UV-sterilized NFMs (15 mg) were incubated with bacterial suspensions (104 CFU mL−1). After growing the bacterial suspensions in the Luria–Bertani (LB) liquid medium (10 mL) with PQZG NFMs, they were illuminated (532 nm, 100 mW/cm2) for 30 min. Growth curves of the bacteria at 37 °C were recorded at preset time intervals according to the UV–vis absorbance measurements at 600 nm. The LB liquid medium without NFMs was used as a control.

The inhibitory activity of PQZG NFMs against bacteria was determined by assessing colony formation. Different UV-sterilized NFMs (15 mg) were incubated with bacterial suspensions (104 CFU mL−1) for 8 h after illumination (532 nm, 100 mW/cm2) for 30 min. After dilution with sterile saline solution, 100 μL of the suspension from the different groups was evenly smeared on LB medium. Subsequently, the culture plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h for colony formation and counting. The antibacterial efficiency of PQZG NFMs was calculated as follows:
(4)
Antibacterial
efficiency
( % )
=
N
C
-
N
S
N
C
× 100 %
where NC and NS are the numbers (CFU mL−1) of bacterial colonies in the blank control and NFMs sample groups, respectively.

Mouse fibroblasts (L929) cells (1 × 104 cells/well) were cultured in the complete medium consisting of Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum in a 96-well plate at 37 °C for 24 h, as previously described [41]. Meanwhile, the UV-sterilized PQZG NFMs were immersed in 5 mL of the DMEM without serum at 37 °C for 24 h to acquire the extract (10 mg/mL). The extract was diluted by the complete medium to the final concentrations of 0, 25, 50, 75, 100, and 150 μg/mL. 100 μL of extracts at these concentrations were added to different wells and then incubated for 24 and 48 h at 37 °C in 5% CO2. Then, 100 μL of the cell counting kit-8 solution was added to each well and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h. Finally, absorbance was determined at a wavelength of 450 nm using a microplate reader to determine the cell viability.

Cell morphology and apoptosis were observed by live/dead staining experiments. The cells were stained with calcein-AM/PI (BestBio BB-4126, China) according to the manufacturer’s instructions, and the samples were observed using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) (FV1200, Olympus).

All experiments were performed in at least three replicate. Data are expressed as the mean ± standard deviation. Statistical analyses were conducted using one-way analysis of variance with the IBM SPSS Statistics software (version 19.0; IBM Corp., USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of NAR@ZIF-8 and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis processes of GDQ and NAR-loaded ZIF-8 are shown in Scheme 1. SEM images revealed that ZIF-8 has a uniform rhombic dodecahedral particle shape, similar to that of NAR@ZIF-8 (Fig. 1 a–d). Average diameters of ZIF-8 and NAR@ZIF-8 particles were 84.9 nm and 129.2 nm, respectively (Fig. 1k–i). The larger particle size of NAR@ZIF-8 demonstrated that NAR was successfully encapsulated into the ZIF-8 crystals, probably due to the introduction of guest molecules during the growth process of ZIF-8 [42]. As calculated from the UV/vis absorbance results, NAR was loaded into ZIF-8 using the one-pot method with a DLC of 22.57% and a DLE of 38.41% at an added concentration of 10 mg/mL−1 (Fig. S1), respectively. Citric acid is a facile carbon source for the production of GQDs containing abundant small sp2 clusters [36]. As shown in the TEM image, the prepared GQDs were mostly spherical with an average diameter of 8.1 nm (Fig. 1i–j).

To further confirm the generation of GQDs, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded (Fig. S2). An evident absorption at approximately 365 nm was observed in the GQDs, while citric acid showed only UV absorption below 300 nm, indicating the uniform size of the sp2 clusters in the GQDs [36]. Photoluminescence (PL) excitation spectrum indicates that the total excitation region of the GQDs is between 325 and 425 nm (Fig. 2 a). PL emission spectra showed that the emission wavelength of the GQDs was excitation-dependent. The emission peak red-shifted from 440 to 490 nm as the excitation wavelength increased from 300 to 420 nm (Fig. 2b). This phenomenon is mainly due to the oxidation of the GQDs and surface defects [43]. Furthermore, the GQDs solution emitted intense blue light when irradiated with 365 nm UV light (Fig. 2a), indicating its abundant sp2 clusters and fluorescent properties.

Characteristic peaks of NAR and ZIF-8 were observed for NAR@ZIF-8 (Fig. 2c). The peak at 1640 cm−1 of C=O in NAR retained in NAR@ZIF-8, but the peak at 1250 cm−1 corresponding to C-O-C of NAR disappeared in NAR@ZIF-8 ascribed to the shielding effect of the ZIF-8. XRD showed that NAR@ZIF-8, with sharp diffraction peaks, had high crystallinity similar to that of pure ZIF-8 nanoparticles (Fig. 2d). The characteristic diffraction peaks at 2θ of 7.31°, 10.35°, 12.67°, and 18.01° were found in the XRD patterns of NAR@ZIF-8, indicating NAR@ZIF-8 with a typical ZIF-8 framework structure. In addition, NAR dispersed in ZIF-8 induced a slight decrease in the peak intensity of NAR@ZIF-8, and its characteristic diffraction peaks disappeared, indicating that ZIF-8 impeded the crystal formation of NAR and that NAR was loaded into the ZIF-8 pores at the molecular level. The elemental composition and chemical states of ZIF-8 and NAR@ZIF-8 were examined using XPS. The characteristic peaks of the full XPS survey spectra demonstrated that ZIF-8 and NAR@ZIF-8 mainly consisted of C, N, O, and Zn (Fig. 2e). A slight shift in the binding energies for Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 in ZIF-8 was observed upon loading NAR (from 1021.78 and 1044.89 eV to 1021.54 and 1044.62 eV) (Fig. 2f), indicating the presence of weak binding or coordination between the unsaturated sites of Zn in ZIF-8 and NAR [44]. The deconvoluted C 1 s spectra were divided into two peaks at 284.09 and 285.26 eV, which corresponded to the groups of C-C/C=C and C-N, respectively (Fig. 2g). Compared to ZIF-8, a new characteristic peak existed at 286.94 eV in NAR@ZIF-8, which correlated with the carbonyl groups (C=O), further suggesting the successful encapsulation of NAR.

N2 adsorption–desorption experiments were performed to evaluate the porosity of the as-synthesized nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 2i, both ZIF-8 and NAR@ZIF-8 exhibited type-I isotherm curves, indicating microporous properties [45]. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface areas and pore volumes of NAR@ZIF-8 (1853.31 m2/g and 1.40 cm3/g) were lower than those of ZIF-8 (2235.35 m2/g and 1.58 cm3/g, respectively; Fig. S3a). This is possibly because the loaded NAR occupied the cavities and channels of the ZIF-8 framework. The pore width distribution results further confirmed the decrease in mesopores with the loading of the target molecules (Fig. S3b, c). Moreover, TGA curves revealed the larger weight loss of NAR@ZIF-8 up to 800 °C compared to that of ZIF-8 (Fig. S4), revealing the decomposition of the loaded NAR molecules and providing further evidence of successful encapsulation of NAR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of PQZG NFMs
ce:section.ce:para: FE-SEM images of PQ and PQZG nanofibers are shown in Fig. 3 a–d. PQ with a uniform fibrillar structure was a suitable matrix for preparing nanomaterial-incorporated composite fibers (Fig. 3a and e). Average diameter of PQ fibers increased from 63.97 nm to 89.78 (PQZG5), 112.07 (PQZG10), and 105.63 nm (PQZG15) with the addition of GQDs and NAR@ZIF-8 (Fig. S6). Incorporation of GQDs and NAR@ZIF-8 alters the conductivity, viscosity, and surface tension of the spinning solution, thereby affecting the morphology and diameter of the fibers [46,47]. The presence of NAR@ZIF-8 within the fibers was further confirmed via TEM (Fig. 3f–h). CLSM was used to evaluate the photoluminescent behaviors of the fibers. As shown in Fig. S7, PQZG fibers exhibited homogeneous blue fluorescence, and the intensity increased with increasing GQD content, indicating that the PL properties of GQDs remained in the composite nanofibers. Additionally, EDS analysis revealed that the elemental mapping of Zn in PQZG15 fibers confirmed the presence of NAR@ZIF-8 particles (Fig. 3i–m).

As shown in Fig. 4 a, the PQZG contains the characteristic peaks of NAR@ZIF-8 FTIR spectra associated with the imidazole ring at 759.1 cm−1 and the C=C ring stretching at 1579.5 cm−1. The O–H stretching vibration transferred to lower frequencies (3375.0 cm−1) in PQZG15, indicating the formation of hydrogen bonding between O and H in PQ fibers and N–H in NAR@ZIF-8. In the XRD patterns of PQZG (Fig. S8), the intensities of the characteristic peaks at 7.31°, 10.35°, and 12.67° increased with NAR@ZIF-8 addition, revealing the effective incorporation and preservation of the crystal structure in the composite nanofibrous membranes. From TGA curves (Fig. 4b), the PQZG membranes showed different degradation behavior from that of PQ membranes after 200 °C due to the decomposition of NAR@ZIF-8 and GQDs. Moreover, the residual weight of NFMs after 800 °C degradation changed from 5.00 of PQ to 8.82 (PQZG5), 11.97 (PQZG10), and 15.12% (PQZG15), which had a positive correlation with the addition of NAR@ZIF-8. Fig. 4c–f shows the mechanical properties of the PQ and PQZG NFMs. PQZG15 showed the highest elastic modulus and tensile strength, but the lowest elongation at break, suggesting mechanical reinforcement and rigidity improvement after the incorporation of NAR@ZIF-8 and GQDs. Fig. 4g presents the kinetics of the WCA for PQ and PQZG NFMs. WCA of PQ increased from 27.9° to 41.7°, 52.8°, and 56.5° after the addition of NAR@ZIF-8 and GQDs, respectively. This is probably associated with the surface roughness and energy of PQZG [48]. Additionally, PQZG showed lower WVP and water absorption than PQ (Fig. 4h–i) and the lowest values were observed for PQZG15.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-triggered release of NAR from PQZG NFMs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the potential of the composite NFMs as drug delivery systems, the cumulative percentages of NAR released from PQZG15 at different pH values (5.0, 6.5, and 7.4) were determined and shown in Fig. 5 a. PGZG15 exhibited a slow release of NAR at pH 7.4, approximately 6.98% and 22.37% for 4 and 120 h, respectively. However, a stimulated rapid release of NAR of 32.24% and 17.16% at the initial 4 h was monitored in PBS of pH 5.0 and 6.5, respectively, followed by a relatively slower stage of 64.84% and 49.68% for 120 h. Interestingly, the speed of NAR release at 60 h increased rapidly when the pH of the release medium changed from 7.4 to 6.5 or 5.0 (dotted line), further revealing the pH-responsive behavior of drug release. The NAR/PQ membranes without ZIF-8 showed no significant differences in the release profiles in different PBS media (Fig. 5b). As illustrated in Fig. 5c, the acid-sensitive ZIF-8 structure is corroded and degraded by H+ under low pH conditions, resulting in the release of NAR molecules from the ZIF-8 core [49,50]. Moreover, the TEM images visually demonstrate the degradation process of ZIF-8 under different pH conditions. NAR@ZIF-8 maintained its size and crystal structure after immersion in PBS (pH 7.4) for 60 min (Fig. 5d). However, smaller sizes and structural disintegration were observed after immersion at pH 6.5 and 5.0 for 60 min (Fig. 5e–f). Ultimately, the original rhombic dodecahedral shape of the NAR@ZIF-8 nanoparticles was almost invisible at pH 5.0 after 120 min (Fig. 5g).

NAR release mechanism was investigated following the classic Higuchi and Korsmeyer–Peppas models. As shown in Table 1. , R2 for the kinetic parameters revealed that the data for NAR release were better fitted with the Korsmeyer–Peppas model than with the Higuchi model. Release exponent n values were 0.202, 0.305, and 0.319, respectively, all below 0.43, indicating that the NAR release process mainly corresponds to the Fickian diffusion mechanism [51]. Additionally, the rate constant k increased with decreasing pH, which was consistent with the release behavior. These results suggest that the prepared composite NFMs are promising drug delivery carriers for NAR release in acidic microenvironments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoexcited NFMs with antibacterial effects promote ROS generation
ce:section.ce:para: Photodynamic antibacterial activity is mainly associated with ROS generation, because ROS can effectively disrupt membranous cells, resulting in cell damage and bacterial death. Generation of singlet oxygen (1O2) upon irradiation was detected using a DPBF probe to evaluate the photodynamic efficacy of PQZG NFMs. As shown in Fig. 6 , different degrees of reduction in the UV absorption intensity of DPBF incubated with PQZG NFMs were observed when exposed to light irradiation. As illustrated in Fig. 6a, ROS can oxidize DPBF via an irreversible ring-opening reaction to form colorless endoperoxides and bleach its solution [52], which weakens the DPBF absorption peak at 412 nm. Photooxidation of DPBF by PQZG NFMs, as reflected by the decrease in this peak intensity, depends on the ROS yield upon irradiation. Fig. 6a–c shows the photocatalytic efficacies of the prepared PQZG NFMs. The peak intensity at 412 nm decreased as the incorporation of GQDs increased with the irradiation duration, indicating that the ROS yield was proportional to the irradiation time and the concentration of GQDs in PQZG NFMs. A relatively slight change in the DPBF absorption was observed in the control group, confirming that singlet oxygen generation was dominantly induced by the PQZG composite NFMs. As shown in the normalized curves, the peak intensities of DPBF at 10 min caused by PQZG5, PQZG10, and PQZG15 decreased to 86.2%, 81.5%, and 72.4%, with linear fitting slopes of –0.007, –0.014, and –0.019, respectively. These results demonstrate that the GQD-integrated PQ nanocomposite membranes were successfully endowed with the singlet oxygen generation ability activated by light irradiation. As shown in the energy transfer mechanism (Fig. 6f), 1O2 was generated by energy transfer from the excited triplet state of GQDs to ground-state oxygen under photoexcitation. The electronic density and charge transfer ability affect the catalytic activity and ROS generation of GQDs [15,16]. GQDs possess high 1O2 quantum yield and good biocompatibility [53]. Moreover, previous research reported that ZIF-8 also possesses ROS generation capacity due to the photoelectrons trapped at zinc ion centers within the ZIF-8 framework via ligand-to-metal charge transfer [54,55]. Overall, the performance of the photoactive PQZG nanocomposites containing GQDs emphasizes their applicability in aPDT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual strategies of NFM antibacterial activity in light and darkness
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the antibacterial activity of the as-prepared NFMs, bacteriostasis experiments were performed against gram-negative E. coli and gram-positive S. aureus with or without light irradiation using growth inhibition and standard plate counting assays. As shown in Fig. 7 a, b, the proliferation rate of bacteria was significantly decreased after exposure to PQ NFMs compared to that in the control group owing to the positive charge of quaternized chitosan damaging the cellular membrane and inactivating the bacteria [56]. After light irradiation, PQ showed no significant difference in the proliferation rate of the bacteria, indicating that PQ did not exhibit any photodynamic activity. However, PQZG NFMs incorporated with NAR@ZIF-8 and GQDs exhibited higher antimicrobial activities than PQ because of the significant decline in the proliferation rates of S. aureus and E. coli. Notably, we observed a significant reduction in the growth curves of the tested bacteria treated with PQZG NFMs under illumination with simulated light. Formation of bacterial colonies in the PQ and PQZG groups (Fig. 7c) followed the trend of the growth curves. Antibacterial efficiencies of PQ and PQZG NFMs against S. aureus were 26.2% and 74.5% without irradiation, and 29.4% and 94.6% with irradiation, respectively. For the E. coli strain, the antibacterial efficiencies of PQ and PQZG NFMs were 23.5% and 69.7% without irradiation, and 25.8% and 91.3% with irradiation, respectively. Therefore, the presence of NAR@ZIF-8 and GQDs improved the antibacterial activity of PQZG nanocomposite fibers. In an acidic microenvironment, pH-triggered release of NAR molecules can damage the integrity of cellular membranes and increase their permeability, leading to bacterial inactivation [34]. Besides, it has also been reported that Zn2+ originating from the degradation of NAR@ZIF-8 has antibacterial effects [57]. Additionally, photoinduced generation of 1O2 by GQDs in PQZG can affect cellular membranes and contents, causing cytoplasmic leakage and apoptosis in microorganisms [58,59]. Here, ROS generation and antibacterial agent release were the dominant mechanisms underlying the synergetic chemical-photodynamic antibacterial effects of PQZG NFMs, suggesting that the developed nanocomposite membranes exhibit continuous antibacterial activity with or without light.

After determining the inhibitory effect of composite NFMs on bacteria, we further demonstrated the biocompatibility of PQZG as a promising biocidal function membrane on L929 fibroblast cells. Fig. 8 a–h shows the viabilities of L929 cells incubated with PQ and PQZG NFMs after 24 and 48 h. Cell viabilities after 24-h incubation were 88.3%, 89.2%, and 91.1% for PQZG5, PQZG10, and PQZG15, respectively, at a concentration of 150 μg mL−1. The viability was concentration-dependent and was nearly 90% for all PQZG groups, confirming the safety and good biocompatibility of the prepared membranes. After 48 h of incubation, the cell viabilities of PQZG5, PQZG10, and PQZG15 increased to 95%–106%, probably because of cell proliferation on the films, indicating that the PQZG membranes were biocompatible. Cell proliferation and viability are essential factors for the biocompatibility of developed materials. Electrospun nanofibrous membranes with 3D structures and large specific surface areas are excellent 3D physical scaffolds for cell survival and proliferation [60].

Fluorescent images of live/dead staining were recorded to further evaluate the cell survival and proliferation in the developed membranes. Figs. S9 and 8i show the distributions of live and dead cells stained with green fluorescent calcein-AM and red fluorescent PI after 24 and 48 h of incubation, respectively. Strong green and faint red fluorescence were easily distinguished in the fluorescent images, implying numerous live and very few dead cells, respectively. Moreover, agglomeration of L929 fibroblasts was observed after 48 h of incubation than after 24 h, confirming cell proliferation after co-culture with PQZG, which is consistent with the above qualitative results of cell viability. Therefore, the prepared PQZG nanocomposite membranes can be used as non-cytotoxic and bio-safe materials for antibacterial bio-protection.

Herein, we developed a dual stimuli-responsive fibrous nanoplatform, PQZG, using PQ composite nanofibers incorporated with NAR@ZIF-8 and GQDs that exhibited efficient antibacterial activity. Under acidic conditions, NFM nanoplatforms exhibited pH-triggered drug release. Concomitantly, NFMs responded to light irradiation with the generation of ROS owing to the presence of GQD photosensitizer. Comprehensive characterization revealed that the addition of NAR@ZIF-8 and GQDs enhanced the thermal and mechanical properties of PQZG. In vitro release experiments and microstructures confirmed that NAR was efficiently loaded into ZIF-8. PQZG NFMs facilitated pH-triggered NAR release due to the acid-sensitive ZIF-8 crystal structure. Moreover, incorporation of GQDs significantly improved the photoinduced generation of singlet oxygen by PQZG NFMs. Synergetic chemical-photodynamic effects remarkably improved the antibacterial efficacy of NFMs with good biocompatibility. Our findings can facilitate the development of stimuli-responsive NFM nanoplatforms integrated with multiple antibacterial patterns to form a nanofibrous system that exhibits continuous antibacterial activity for day/night bio-protection. We hope that this NFM has potential applications in bio-protective clothing, food packaging material, or wound dressing.
